title: Switzerland Talks Open Amid Hormuz Closure, Lebanon Ceasefire Shatters, Starmer Set to Resign
subhead: US-Iran negotiations resume in Switzerland but Iran's second closure of the Strait of Hormuz and Israeli strikes killing at least 20 in Lebanon after a ceasefire underscore how fragile the peace remains. Meanwhile, Keir Starmer appears set to resign as UK Prime Minister, and Ukraine warns of a massive incoming Russian attack.

sections.heading: Switzerland Talks Begin as Iran Closes Hormuz Again
sections.body:
US and Iranian negotiators convened in Switzerland on Sunday for the first substantive talks since the 14-point memorandum of understanding was signed at Versailles on June 18. Vice President JD Vance arrived Saturday, joined by special envoys Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff. Iran's delegation, led by parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, also landed in Switzerland. Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif and army chief Field Marshal Asim Munir traveled as mediators, with Qatari representatives also participating.

But the opening was anything but smooth. Iran's joint military command declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again on Saturday, citing the US 'clear breach of its commitments' by failing to end the war on all fronts — specifically, the ongoing Israeli operations in Lebanon. Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei warned that if key commitments are not upheld, 'the memorandum of understanding as a whole will be jeopardized.' US Central Command disputed the closure claim, stating that 55 merchant ships transited the strait on Saturday carrying more than 17 million barrels of oil. Trump responded by threatening to impose American tolls on the waterway — what he called 'Guardian Angel' tolls — if a final deal is not reached within 60 days. The interim agreement guarantees toll-free passage for 60 days.
sections.whyItMatters: The talks are the first real test of whether the Versailles MoU can translate into a durable agreement. Iran's immediate weaponization of the strait closure as a pressure tactic shows that Hormuz remains its primary lever, and the 60-day clock is already ticking against a backdrop of mutual distrust.

sections.heading: Lebanon Ceasefire Collapses Within Hours
sections.body:
Israel and Hezbollah agreed to a ceasefire in Lebanon on Friday, brokered with help from US, Iranian, and Qatari mediators. But within hours, Israeli strikes killed at least 20 people in southern Lebanon, according to Reuters. Hezbollah accused Israel of committing hundreds of ceasefire violations. Lebanon's state media reported at least seven people killed, including two children, in the strikes on Tyre and other southern towns. Lebanese conservationist Mona Khalil, 76, known for her sea turtle advocacy, also died from injuries sustained in an Israeli attack on her home.

The Israeli operations in Lebanon are the direct trigger for Iran's second closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has maintained from the outset that Lebanon is integral to any ceasefire — the MoU calls for a halt to military operations on all fronts, including Lebanon. A Hezbollah official told the AP that Iran informed the group it would not reopen the strait until Israel publicly commits to a 'comprehensive ceasefire' in Lebanon and an end to military operations there.
sections.whyItMatters: The Lebanon ceasefire was supposed to be the prerequisite that got the Iran talks back on track after Friday's postponement. Its near-immediate collapse makes a mockery of the MoU's 'all fronts' language and hands Iran a justification for escalatory moves like the Hormuz closure. Every hour the fighting continues in Lebanon, the odds of the Switzerland talks producing substantive results diminish.

sections.heading: Ukraine Warns of Massive Russian Attack as Poland-Ukraine Relations Fray
sections.body:
President Volodymyr Zelensky warned on Saturday that Russian forces were preparing an imminent massive attack on Ukraine, urging residents to take shelter. Russian strikes across multiple regions killed at least six people the same day. Zelensky also confirmed a Ukrainian drone strike on an oil refinery in Russia's Tyumen Region, western Siberia — more than 2,000 km from Ukraine — demonstrating Kyiv's continued ability to reach deep into Russian territory.

For the second consecutive day, Zelensky urged Belarus to dismantle relay stations he said were being used to stage Russian drone attacks on Ukrainian territory. Meanwhile, a diplomatic rift with Poland deepened: Polish President Karol Nawrocki stripped Zelensky of Poland's highest honor, the Order of the White Eagle, citing a dispute over Ukraine's renaming of a military unit after WWII-era Ukrainian insurgents accused of massacring Poles. Zelensky responded by returning the decoration, stating it was 'meant for the Ukrainian People and our army.' Three former Ukrainian presidents also returned their Polish orders of merit, and Zelensky's chief of staff and foreign minister renounced their awards.

Polish Foreign Minister Radoslaw Sikorski warned that 'the winner of the war of history and orders can only be Moscow,' while Defense Minister Wladyslaw Kosiniak-Kamysz said relations were 'heading in a very bad direction.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Poland-Ukraine dispute is a gift to Moscow at a moment when Kyiv needs Warsaw's support more than ever. Zelensky's warning of a massive incoming attack, combined with depleted Western air defense stockpiles (exacerbated by the Iran war), means Ukraine faces heightened vulnerability precisely when its closest ally relationship is under strain.

sections.heading: Starmer Expected to Resign as UK Prime Minister
sections.body:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer is expected to announce his resignation as early as Monday, according to a report in The Observer. The report said Starmer has been holding discussions with cabinet colleagues, advisers, donors, and trade union leaders, and concluded his position may no longer be sustainable. Government sources pushed back, insisting Starmer remains focused on governing. Just days earlier, Starmer had publicly declared: 'I am not going to walk away.'

Andy Burnham, the Greater Manchester Mayor who won a special election in Makerfield this week, is widely seen as the most likely successor. Burnham is reportedly not planning an immediate challenge but will return to Westminster while political pressure on Starmer builds. Australia's Prime Minister Anthony Albanese warned that 'history is not positive' about leadership spills, referencing the UK's six prime ministers in ten years — none of whom served a full term.
sections.whyItMatters: If confirmed, Starmer's departure would make him the latest in a long line of short-tenured British leaders, deepening the UK's political instability. With NATO defense spending debates intensifying (Hegseth's troop review this week) and the Iran deal's economic consequences still unfolding, a UK leadership vacuum comes at an awkward time for Western coordination.

sections.heading: Hegseth Orders US Troop Review in Europe, Threatens NATO Dues
sections.body:
US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced a new review of America's troop deployments in Europe on Thursday and threatened to withhold some US dues to NATO if 'free riding' allies do not meet defense spending commitments. The announcement came during a Brussels visit where Hegseth also met with Poland's defense minister, who said the Pentagon is open to hosting a permanent US base in Poland. Lithuania's reshuffled coalition government, meanwhile, signed a cabinet agreement seeking a long-term US military presence in the NATO country bordering Russia and Belarus.

The review follows the Trump administration's broader pattern of questioning US security commitments. The Iran war consumed significant munitions stockpiles — acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao told Congress in May that the US was 'doing a pause' on arms sales to Taiwan to ensure sufficient munitions for the Iran conflict, though Hegseth later walked that back. A Reason analysis this week detailed how the US burned through interceptors shooting down Iranian drones and missiles, leaving air defense stockpiles dangerously low, with Israel's interceptor count reportedly falling to 'double digits' before the ceasefire.
sections.whyItMatters: The combination of a European troop review and depleted US munitions stockpiles creates a compounding problem for NATO's eastern flank. Frontline states like Poland and Lithuania are scrambling to lock in permanent US bases precisely as Washington signals it may reduce its footprint — and at a moment when Ukraine is pleading for more Patriot systems that the US can no longer readily supply.

sections.heading: Spain's Political Crisis Deepens as PM's Wife Ordered to Stand Trial
sections.body: A Spanish judge ruled on Saturday that Begona Gomez, the wife of Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez, must stand trial on corruption charges and has been banned from leaving the country, with her passport revoked. The case, which involves allegations of influence peddling, has been a simmering political crisis for Spain's Socialist government. Sanchez had previously considered resigning over the investigation last year before deciding to stay on. The ruling escalates pressure on a government already dealing with the EU's internal disputes over Russia sanctions and the long-term budget.
sections.whyItMatters: A sitting European prime minister's spouse facing a corruption trial is a serious distraction for Spain at a moment when the EU needs cohesion on defense spending, budget negotiations, and the Russia dossier. If the case forces Sanchez out, it adds another leadership vacancy to a Europe already losing Starmer and struggling to present a united front.

bottomLine: The Versailles agreement is facing its first real stress test and it is not going well. Iran reopened the Hormuz card within 72 hours of signing the deal, using it as leverage over the Lebanon front. Israeli strikes killed at least 20 people in Lebanon hours after a ceasefire was announced, validating Iran's claim that the 'all fronts' ceasefire was not being honored. Switzerland talks are underway, but both sides are signaling maximalist positions: Iran demands full compliance before substantive negotiations, and Trump is already threatening US tolls on Hormuz shipping. Elsewhere, the US arsenal gap from the Iran war is compounding NATO's eastern vulnerability, Ukraine faces a looming Russian offensive with its Poland relationship in tatters, and two European leaders — Starmer and potentially Sanchez — may be on their way out.
